title: Doom
notes: Mouse is slightly wonky, but excellent otherwise. Perfectly playable.

title: NieR: Automata
notes: Had to create "My Documents" folder in ~/.local/share/Steam/SteamApps/compatdata/524220/pfx/dosdevices/c:/users/steamuser in order to hold settings and saves. Also had to add "PROTON_USE_WINED3D11=1 %command%" into launch options, otherwise, some objects and enemies blinks. Experimented some slowdowns (20fps) when in big areas.

title: NieR:Automata™
notes: Using Proton 3.7-4 beta, the game is in a playable state. At 1440p, I can stay at 45 fps + on a mix of high/medium settings. It only drops in very intense fights, but this probably has to do more with the port itself.

title: NieR:Automata™
notes: It works okay. A bit on the sluggish side, and the graphics settings are a bit glitchy and can cause the game to crash. Change them on the title screen to avoid most issues.

cpu: AMD A10-7850K APU with Radeon R7 Graphics

title: NieR:Automata™

notes:
Plays perfectly on openSUSE Tumbleweed (a tested rolling distro) with an important tweak: you must upgrade to 18.2+ that's build with LLVM7. 

LLVM7 is included (along side LLVM6) in TW, but you must build Mesa against it. Luckily this is pretty easy and mostly automated for you. Use openSUSE free OBS service. 

1) create a subproject in your OBS account 

2) branch Mesa from the X11:Xorg project, this will give you a newer Mesa version 

3) open .spec files and change BuildRequires from llvm (which defaults to 6) to llvm7 and also change BuildRequires scons (which also defaults to 6) to scons7. All in all you're changing 2 or 3 lines in both spec files. That's it. Wait 30 or so minutes for OBS to build all the Mesa rpm files and then 

4) add your mesa subproject on OBS to your repo list either via zypper or YaST and 

5) zypper dup --allow-vendor-change --from <name_of_your_mesa_repo>
title: NieR:Automata™

notes: had to add my self to the video group "sudo usermod -a -G video user" frame rate about 5-20 less then window on my hardware
title: NieR:Automata™

notes:
Works perfectly, no graphical glitches, no audio problems, performance is a little worse than the Windows version, also the FAR mod isn't compatible with Linux, so you can't disable global illumination to get a huge performance boost. Not recommended on lower end systems, unless you play at a lower resolution and are fine with 30 fps.

I'm playing it at 1440p with my RX 580 and I get over 30fps at all times, but can't hold a steady 60fps when there's a lot of effects loading in.
title: NieR:Automata™

notes: The game runs perfectly. Same performance issues as running it on Windows. FAR not tested, not likely to work anyway.

title: NieR:Automata™

notes: Steady 60fps on high settings (set your CPU governor to performance). Game crashes semi-randomly. Rarely, but sometimes freezes while viewing the map. When window is moved between workspaces the pod starts firing infinitely. While this feature may be convenient in battle, this can cause misunderstandings between yourself and friendly NPCs or the wildlife.

title: NieR:Automata™

notes:
After the initial system data loading, it seems to be stuck with audio playing. Alt+tab out and in gets to the main screen perfectly fine though.

Plays perfectly fine on 1080p with low settings.

Get about 18 fps or so on 1440p with low settings.

Hangs if i change it to high settings on either of those resolutions. Have to force close wine.

Also, when switching resolutions while in game, i have to alt+tab in an out with every change as well.

title: NieR:Automata™

notes: Runs great, if not better than on windows with less stuttering. However; I've came across a really annoying visual bug where sometimes the lights and reflections are way too bright, like water and surface reflections and getting a video call over the pod. I believe that this bug is caused by the game going into a cutscene and going back into the game. Asides from that, it runs fine.
